caveats: a: The occupation source is NAICS 315, not 315210.
a: GenAI exposure is not the same as feasible labor substitution, especially on a shop floor.
a: The estimate excludes conventional non-AI sewing automation unless AI is integral to perception, planning, or control.
rho: No representative survey measures five-year AI implementation among 315210 firms.
rho: Implementation may augment scarce staff rather than reduce payroll.
rho: The frozen compensation ratio uses a 2024/2022 vintage mismatch and a cross-code harmonization adjustment.
e: NAICS classification establishes activity, not repeat revenue quality or transferability.
e: The frozen n=44 is a margin-bridged estimate based on broader manufacturing economics, not a directly observed LMM count.
s5: Neither source isolates NAICS 315210 or the $1-10 million EBITDA band.
s5: BizBuySell's 2025 clothing-and-fabric median cash flow was only $100,000, far below the target band.
s5: Owner age does not imply a sale, and internal succession is not automatically a qualifying transfer.
q: Public pricing policies are illustrative, not representative.
q: Customer-owned materials and frequent style changes can make labor economics unusually transparent.
q: This primitive excludes volume loss and implementation costs.
d5: BLS output is NAICS 315, not 315210, and reflects the full 2024-2034 decade.
d5: Output projections combine end-demand, trade, sourcing, and productivity effects rather than measuring the current contractor service basket directly.
d5: The base uses the published compound annual rate, not a six-digit forecast.
o: The estimate concerns operator requirement, not whether the operator is domestic or how many workers it employs.
o: Simple, standardized garments may automate faster than high-mix or delicate-material work.
o: Customers could vertically integrate production even though software alone cannot supply the service.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Business-to-business contract manufacturing, usually style-, batch-, or unit-priced, with separate development, sampling, and production stages and customer-owned designs and often materials.
lens.excluded_activities: Apparel manufacturers that buy their own fabric and sell finished goods, captive sewing units, one-person tailoring, non-apparel textile fabrication, embroiderers classified elsewhere, shells, and non-control financing vehicles.
lens.included_activities: US cut-and-sew contractors in the lower-middle-market band that repeatedly cut, sew, finish, sample, or otherwise assemble apparel from materials owned by external brand, uniform, institutional, or designer customers.

a.bridge: BLS three-digit apparel-manufacturing employment and O*NET sewing tasks proxy the six-digit contractor occupation/task mix. Sewing-machine operators alone are 25,280 of roughly 74,000 apparel-manufacturing jobs, while their core tasks require manipulating fabric and monitoring stitches; the ILO finds GenAI exposure concentrated in clerical work. I therefore assign high exposure to office/customer-documentation and planning tasks, moderate exposure to pattern and quality-analysis tasks, and low exposure to hands-on sewing, cutting, pressing, handling, and supervision. The broader NAICS-315 mix likely overstates design and headquarters work relative to contractors, biasing exposure upward.
caveats: The occupation source is NAICS 315, not 315210.
GenAI exposure is not the same as feasible labor substitution, especially on a shop floor.
The estimate excludes conventional non-AI sewing automation unless AI is integral to perception, planning, or control.
a.change_evidence: A current six-digit OEWS staffing table paired with task-level time studies at 10-20 US contractors, including current software and vision-system use, would materially tighten the range.
a.rationale: Most contractor payroll is tied to physical, variable-fabric production that text-and-image AI cannot directly perform. The credible exposed wedge is production planning, quote and tech-pack interpretation, customer service, purchasing coordination, scheduling, inspection documentation, and some computer-vision-assisted quality control.

d5.bridge: BLS projects real output for the broader US apparel-manufacturing industry to fall from $12.3 billion in 2024 to $10.1 billion in 2034, a -1.9% annual rate. Applying that published constant-dollar annual rate for five years gives approximately 0.91. Contractors could underperform if work continues moving offshore, or outperform through domestic small-batch, compliance-sensitive, speed-to-market, uniform, and specialty demand, so the range is wider than the mechanical proxy.
caveats: BLS output is NAICS 315, not 315210, and reflects the full 2024-2034 decade.
Output projections combine end-demand, trade, sourcing, and productivity effects rather than measuring the current contractor service basket directly.
The base uses the published compound annual rate, not a six-digit forecast.
d5.change_evidence: Five-year constant-price shipment histories and booked-order data for 315210, split by fashion, uniform, medical/institutional, government, and specialty customers, would materially improve the demand estimate.
d5.rationale: The base reflects continued contraction in domestic apparel output rather than unit demand growth. The contractor niche still has defensible demand where proximity, short runs, responsiveness, IP control, or institutional specifications matter.

caveats: NAICS classification establishes activity, not repeat revenue quality or transferability.
The frozen n=44 is a margin-bridged estimate based on broader manufacturing economics, not a directly observed LMM count.
e.change_evidence: A firm-level screen of the 44 frozen LMM candidates using customer contracts, revenue recurrence, customer concentration, ownership independence, and owner-dependence interviews would directly measure eligibility.
e.rationale: The NAICS definition itself is centered on establishments that cut or sew materials owned by others, so repeat outsourced service is the dominant model. The discount allows for project-only shops, economically captive operations, severe customer concentration, owner-dependent relationships, or firms without a transferable operating platform.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 315210 ($953,832,017) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 315210 ($2,588,843,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1525 (margins.json[31]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Food Processing EBITDA/Sales 15.25% (dominant subsector of NAICS 31)); revenue/employee $104,977 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 315210); class <5: 1,348 firms, avg revenue/firm $267,818 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $40,842 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 415 firms, avg revenue/firm $585,441 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $89,280 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 283 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,553,057 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $236,841 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 210 firms, avg revenue/firm $3,969,533 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $605,354 -> out of $1-10M band; class 100-499: 35 firms, avg revenue/firm $13,914,429 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $2,121,950 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 9 firms, avg revenue/firm $24,971,667 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $3,808,179 -> IN $1-10M band; n_band = 44

caveats: The estimate concerns operator requirement, not whether the operator is domestic or how many workers it employs.
Simple, standardized garments may automate faster than high-mix or delicate-material work.
Customers could vertically integrate production even though software alone cannot supply the service.
o.change_evidence: Commercial deployment data showing end-to-end autonomous cutting, feeding, sewing, finishing, inspection, and style changeover across representative garment types would be the decisive evidence for a lower operator-required share.
o.rationale: The service basket physically transforms customer-owned materials into apparel and carries responsibility for fit, stitch quality, delivery, rework, and compliance. Software can improve planning and inspection, but deformable-fabric handling and sewing remain physical tasks; even substantially automated production still requires an accountable manufacturing operator rather than software alone.

caveats: Public pricing policies are illustrative, not representative.
Customer-owned materials and frequent style changes can make labor economics unusually transparent.
This primitive excludes volume loss and implementation costs.
q.change_evidence: Five years of quote revisions, gross margins, open-book clauses, rebid frequency, customer churn, and realized price changes at several LMM contractors would directly measure pass-through and retention.
q.rationale: Contract manufacturing is quoted by project, stage, batch, or unit; one US contractor states that costs are re-estimated at each stage and quotes expire after 15 days. That makes labor savings visible and contestable at repeat bidding and renewal, so customers and competitors should capture much of the benefit. A contractor can retain more where savings improve speed, small-batch economics, reliability, or quality rather than merely lowering direct labor cost.

caveats: No representative survey measures five-year AI implementation among 315210 firms.
Implementation may augment scarce staff rather than reduce payroll.
The frozen compensation ratio uses a 2024/2022 vintage mismatch and a cross-code harmonization adjustment.
rho.change_evidence: Vendor deployment records and before/after labor-hour data from US apparel contractors using AI scheduling, tech-pack parsing, automated inspection, or customer-service systems would materially change implementation confidence.
rho.rationale: Within the relatively small exposed wedge, document drafting, scheduling support, customer communications, and exception triage are implementable within five years. Realized substitution will be held back by small-firm systems, inconsistent tech packs and order data, changing styles, quality accountability, and the need for human approval. Core sewing remains difficult because fabric is deformable and the O*NET tasks are physical.

s5.bridge: Census research reports 62.4% of manufacturing employer-business owners in its older-owner category, and BizBuySell reports 21 completed 2025 sales for the broader clothing-and-fabric manufacturing category. I treat those as evidence of succession pressure plus an active but thin transfer channel, then bound a five-year qualifying-control-transfer probability below the share of older owners because many firms close, transfer internally, remain unsold, or fall outside the LMM lens. The age proxy may overstate near-term exits; marketplace data undercount private transfers but skew toward much smaller firms.
caveats: Neither source isolates NAICS 315210 or the $1-10 million EBITDA band.
BizBuySell's 2025 clothing-and-fabric median cash flow was only $100,000, far below the target band.
Owner age does not imply a sale, and internal succession is not automatically a qualifying transfer.
s5.change_evidence: Owner-age and succession-intent data for the 44 frozen candidates, plus GF Data, Axial, DealStats, and broker records of 315210 control deals with EBITDA of $1-10 million, would materially move this probability.
s5.rationale: Aging manufacturing ownership supports a meaningful five-year transfer pool, while thin margins, customer concentration, compliance exposure, and the small number of observed clothing/fabric sales limit how many businesses will complete a qualifying control transaction.

principal_driver: High labor intensity combined with a focused, implementable coordination and quality-control AI wedge.
principal_weakness: Most labor is physical sewing while domestic apparel output is projected to shrink and contract pricing promotes pass-through.

sources.supports: Defines contractors as establishments cutting or sewing materials owned by others, distinguishes manufacturers using purchased fabric, and reports 2,094 employer establishments in 2023.
sources.title: Census Bureau profile: 31521 Cut and Sew Apparel Contractors

sources.supports: Reports roughly 73,000-74,000 apparel-manufacturing jobs in spring 2026 and 25,280 sewing-machine operators in 2025, alongside cutting, inspection, and production-supervision occupations.
sources.title: BLS Industry at a Glance: Apparel Manufacturing, NAICS 315

sources.supports: Describes core tasks including positioning items under needles, guiding garments, threading machines, monitoring stitches, and detecting malfunctions.
sources.title: O*NET: Sewing Machine Operators 51-6031.00

sources.supports: Reports that clerical occupations remain the most exposed to GenAI and that exposure represents task transformation potential rather than automatic whole-job removal.
sources.title: ILO: Generative AI and Jobs — A Refined Global Index of Occupational Exposure

sources.supports: Projects apparel-manufacturing real output from $12.3 billion in 2024 to $10.1 billion in 2034, a -1.9% compound annual rate, and employment from 84,500 to 58,800.
sources.title: BLS Table 2.11: Employment and output by industry, 2024-2034

sources.supports: A US cut-and-sew contractor states that it estimates costs and invoices a 50% deposit at each development, sampling, or production stage; quotes are valid for 15 days and customer patterns and designs remain customer property.
sources.title: Made X Hudson: Factory Pricing, Process & Policies

sources.supports: Reports 21 completed clothing-and-fabric manufacturer sales in 2025, with median sale price $150,000, median revenue $414,000, median cash flow $100,000, and 287 median days on market.
sources.title: BizBuySell Insight Report Data Tables: Full-Year 2025

sources.supports: Reports that apparel automation remains challenging because fabrics are deformable and documents staged deployments requiring validation, interoperability, runtime verification, and workforce enablement.
sources.title: A Deployment Case Study in Robotic Apparel Automation

sources.supports: Using 2021 Annual Business Survey data for reference year 2020, reports manufacturing as the leading industry among employer businesses in its older-owner analysis, with a 62.4% share.
sources.title: US Census Bureau CES Working Paper 24-71: The Metamorphosis of Women Business Owners — A Focus on Age

title: Cut and Sew Apparel Contractors
